top_risks: Tagging burden is wildly understated. The design quantifies only the easy step ('roster entry takes 2 minutes') and never quantifies the core interaction. Do the math the design avoids: 90 min x 22 players x ~60% on-camera (panning sideline cam) ~= 1,200 player-visible-minutes; at the design's own admitted ID-switch rate ('every few minutes'), that is ~300-400 raw tracklets, maybe 130-200 after the merge pass. Tagging 11 of your own kids means 60-100 binding decisions plus 'Is this still #7?' confirmations, each requiring squinting at 3-5 thumbnail crops of children in IDENTICAL kits with NO jersey OCR until Phase C. Realistic cost: 30-60 minutes of focused work per match, by a busy volunteer, probably on a phone (the drag-roster-onto-grid UI is desktop-shaped). This is the retention killer and the design never states the number.

Rival-player reports on minors are a legal and club-politics landmine that could detonate the Forest relationship rather than cement it. The product explicitly sells reports on opposing players: named free-text identification + jersey number + heatmap + video thumbnails + performance profile of children, retained 'for the life of the account', with zero consent flow, no DPIA, no deletion tooling, no age gating. 'No face recognition' is a fig leaf - name+number tracking IS identification under UK GDPR (children's data, Art. 8 special protections). FA safeguarding guidance restricts filming youth matches; EPPP rules restrict academies monitoring other academies' players. The first rival parent or county FA complaint becomes an existential incident, and an academy partner (Forest) is MORE exposed than an amateur club, not less.

Pay-before-quality with no automatic remedy for the most common failure. The credit is debited and the GPU burned BEFORE the quality score exists. Kit clash, bibs, pitch-level phone footage, backlight - the design admits all of these degrade output to mush, yet a 'completed but useless' job is explicitly 'a support decision, not automatic' refund. For grassroots footage that is the MODAL bad outcome, not the edge case, and 'support' is a 1-2 eng team. Customers will read this as 'we keep your $25 either way'.

Value-for-money gap for both personas. $25 + 30-60 min of your own labor buys distance +/-10-20%, speed bands, sprints, heatmap - no events, no passes, no goals, no real highlights. Academy buyers (the Forest persona) already have GPS vests giving the same physical metrics at +/-2%; what they lack is the event/tactical layer, which is explicitly out of scope. Amateur buyers comparing against Veo/Trace get full auto-follow video + highlight clips bundled - the '$25-75/match comparable' anchor is misleading because those products deliver the footage experience, not just numbers. What is uniquely valuable here (rival scouting) is exactly the part that is legally radioactive.

Cold-start trust wall: a cost-sensitive, non-technical buyer must buy credits, film correctly per guidelines they have not internalized, upload 6GB, wait 1-4h, and do an hour of tagging before EVER seeing what a report looks like. No sample report, no first-match-free, no money-back is mentioned anywhere in the design. Phase B self-serve conversion will stall at the checkout page.

Silent data corruption via mis-tagging: with identical kits and no OCR in Phase A/B, users WILL bind tracklets to the wrong kid, and the wrong kid's distance/sprints then appear on a TrackedPlayer's public player page as a 'Video analysis' tab with no QA backstop (no team-total-distance sanity check, no anomaly flag). For an academy audience built on the project's no-fabrication principle, one parent spotting their child credited with another kid's numbers is a trust-ending event.

Cost-model fragility: the '$8 floor keeps >50% margin at worst-case $6.45' claim is arithmetically wrong (1 - 6.45/8 = 19%); InferenceSlicer tiling multiplies detection cost ~4x and can push T4 jobs past the 4h assumption into the 5h timeout; the 5% retry budget is inconsistent with a 2-attempt retry policy on flaky grassroots footage; Stripe fees (~$1.03 on a $25 single, 4.1%) are omitted.

Weekend pile-up expectation mismatch: parallelism capped at 3, jobs 1-4h, but the workload is admitted to be 'lumpy weekend' - ten Sunday-evening uploads means the last report lands Monday midday, then still needs an hour of tagging. No SLA or expectation-setting anywhere in the UX.
required_changes: Gut the rival-reports scope for v1: own-team players only, behind an uploader attestation that the club holds media/data consent (academies already collect this); opposition players exist only as anonymous 'Opposition #7' tracklets with no name field, no persistent identity, no report. Add a DPIA, a per-player deletion path (find-and-purge across reports/thumbnails/parquet), and ToS language covering third parties in frame, BEFORE Phase A processes a single youth match. Re-introduce named rival reports only with a consent mechanism, if ever.

Treat tag-review time as a product KPI with a budget (e.g. <=10 min/match for 11 players) and refuse to ship Phase B self-serve until concierge Phase A measures it under budget. Concretely: move jersey OCR from Phase C to Phase B (it is the single biggest tagging accelerator and the design strands it after self-serve launch), default-accept high-confidence auto-bindings so users only review exceptions, and build the review screen mobile-first.

Add a cheap pre-flight quality gate BEFORE the credit debit: sample 2-3 minutes on CPU/cheap GPU at upload-complete, compute a provisional quality score, and hard-warn or block ('similar kits detected - team split will fail; fix or proceed at own risk'). Then make refunds automatic below a completed-job quality threshold instead of 'a support decision' - you already compute the 0-1 score; use it.

Fix the cold-start: publish a full interactive sample report from real consented footage on the marketing page, and give every new team their first match free or unconditionally refundable. The Phase A concierge output is the perfect source for this asset.

Support multi-file uploads and partial-match coverage: amateur recordings are routinely 2+ clips (halftime stop, phone storage splits); the single raw/{team_id}/{match_id}.mp4 blob path assumes one file. Define what a half-match report claims (and costs).

Add a finalize-time QA backstop against mis-tagging: team-total distance plausibility check, per-player minutes-visible vs match-length sanity flags, and an explicit 'low tagging confidence' banner on any TrackedPlayer-page report whose bindings were never human-confirmed.

Correct the unit economics: redo the floor-price math (real floor for >50% margin at $6.45 worst-case COGS is ~$13, not $8), add Stripe fees and a measured (not assumed) retry/quality-refund rate to COGS, and benchmark actual T4 wall-clock with InferenceSlicer enabled in Phase 0 - if slicing pushes jobs past ~4h, the $3.10 compute number and the 5h timeout both break.

Remove or honestly define the 'annotated highlight strip' promised in section 1.1 - the pipeline (section 2.3) explicitly cannot detect events, so there are no 'highlights' to strip together beyond sprint moments; as written it is an internal contradiction that violates the project's own no-fabrication principle.

Define the rest of the money story: refunds for unused pack credits (team folds mid-season, 50-pack buyer churns), chargeback handling when the payer leaves the club, credit handling on user-initiated mid-job cancel, and align the 90-day raw-video deletion with the dispute/re-process window (day-100 complaints currently have no evidence and Phase C model upgrades cannot re-run deleted footage).

Set queue expectations in the UX: show estimated start time based on queue depth at submission, and state a soft SLA ('reports typically ready within X hours on weekends') so Sunday uploaders are not surprised Monday.
cost_check: Per-match compute is plausible but sits on optimistic assumptions. ACA serverless T4 (4 vCPU/16GiB) runs roughly $0.75-0.95/hr fully loaded, so the claimed $3.10/match implies ~3.5h of wall-clock - the top of their own 1-4h range. Sanity check: 90 min at 12.5 fps = ~67,500 analyzed frames; RF-DETR Small on T4 TensorRT FP16 at 25-40 fps is fine alone, but InferenceSlicer 4-tile slicing cuts effective throughput to ~6-10 fps, making detection alone ~2-3h before decode, tracking, SigLIP embedding, homography, and NVENC rendering. The 4h assumption is tight and the 5h timeout + 2-retry policy means one late failure doubles a job to $6+. The stated 5% retry budget is not credible against a 2-attempt policy on grassroots footage. Margin math as published: $25 single - $3.50 COGS = 86% gross, but Stripe takes ~$1.03 (2.9%+$0.30), so real gross is ~82%; 10-pack $200 - $35 - ~$6.10 Stripe = ~79%. Still healthy. The clearly WRONG number is the floor-price claim: '$8/match keeps >50% margin even at worst-case $6.45' - actual margin at $8 vs $6.45 is 19.4%, and after Stripe (~$0.53) it is ~12.7%. The >50%-margin floor at worst-case COGS is ~$13-14. Storage ($0.20 amortized, 6GB Hot 14d -> Cool 76d) and egress (~$0.12 for a ~1.4GB 720p preview if fully streamed) check out. Biggest omissions from COGS: quality refunds (if 1-in-6 first uploads is pitch-level phone footage that produces garbage and gets refunded, effective COGS per PAID match rises ~17%), Phase A concierge labor (eng hours per match dwarf $3.50), labeling cost for 3-5k frames x2 fine-tune rounds, and support time adjudicating 'poor-quality-but-completed' disputes. Verdict on economics: per-match unit economics hold at $25/$20/$15 IF jobs stay <=4h on T4, retries stay near 5%, and quality refunds stay low - all three unproven until Phase 0/A measures them, and the third is the shakiest given real grassroots footage. The Spot path ($0.69-1.30) makes Phase C margins comfortable but does nothing for the launch-phase risk. Also note the customer-side economics the design ignores: the true price per match is $25 + 30-60 min of filming-compliant capture + 6GB upload + 30-60 min tagging, against a $750/season 50-pack for noisy physical metrics a $300/season GPS vest beats on accuracy.
missing: (1) Any GDPR/children's-data treatment: no DPIA, no consent capture, no data-subject deletion tooling, no age gating - despite the product centrally processing identifiable performance data of minors, including opposing teams' minors, retained indefinitely. (2) Any quantification of the tag-review step - the design's only stated time cost is the 2-minute roster entry, which is the trivial part. (3) Pre-flight quality assessment before money is committed; quality_score exists only post-hoc, after debit and GPU spend. (4) Multi-file / partial-match uploads (single blob path assumes one continuous file) and any validation that the uploaded video is the declared match. (5) Behavior when team clustering fails outright (kit clash): does the job 'complete' and deliver garbage, forfeiting the auto-refund? (6) Mobile UX for tag review - the buyer persona lives on a phone; drag-and-drop tracklet binding is described as a desktop interaction. (7) The 'annotated highlight strip' in 1.1 has no supporting pipeline stage - internal contradiction with 2.3. (8) Refund policy for unused pack credits, chargebacks, team dissolution, and user-initiated mid-job cancellation. (9) Metric comparability across model versions - Phase D multi-match trends will silently mix Phase A and Phase C model outputs. (10) League/competition filming-rights restrictions (some youth leagues prohibit recording opposition) - uploader attestation absent from ToS notes. (11) Who staffs 'support decision' refunds and tagging help at Phase B scale. (12) Any SLA or queue-depth expectation setting for the admitted weekend-lumpy workload.
